source.title: Corollary discharge circuits in the primate brain.

confidence_rationale: This is the canonical primate-substrate review of corollary discharge (CD) -- the efference-copy infrastructure that any forward-model comparator architecture (SD-029, MECH-256, and the SD-003-prereq substrate work generally) presupposes. Crapse and Sommer survey single-unit, microstimulation, and inactivation evidence across saccadic, vocalisation, locomotor, and reach systems and arrive at the unifying claim: copies of motor commands are routed to sensory and parietal structures so that predicted sensory consequences can be subtracted from incoming reafference. That is precisely the architectural shape SD-003-prereq has to instantiate -- an efference-copy pathway feeding a forward model whose output is differenced against the realised stream. Confidence is high on source quality (Curr Opin Neurobiol, NIH-funded, widely cited) and moderate-to-high on mapping fidelity: the paper directly evidences the substrate (a_actual must be distributed to the forward model alongside the realised stream), but it stops short of formalising the comparator residual the way MECH-256 does, and it makes no architectural commitment about whether the forward model is single-pass or two-pass.
failure_signatures: When CD pathways are silenced (e.g. inactivation of the MD thalamic relay carrying the saccadic CD), monkeys lose the ability to compensate for self-generated sensory change -- they treat reafferent shifts as if they were externally caused. This is the substrate-level instantiation of the SD-003 failure mode: without an efference-copy input, the comparator cannot distinguish self-caused from world-caused change.

CD signals are distributed across many circuits with different latencies and modality profiles; a single forward model is unlikely to suffice across streams. This supports MECH-256's stream-agnostic framing (one comparator per reafferent stream) over a monolithic E2 comparator.

The review notes that CD does not always equate to a clean efference copy -- some signals are pre-motor predictions, others are post-motor. A comparator architecture that assumes a_actual is the only input may miss intention-level prediction signals that the brain actually uses.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Across primate sensorimotor systems (saccades, vocalisation, reaching, locomotion), copies of motor commands -- corollary discharges -- are routed to sensory, parietal, and frontal structures, where they enable prediction of the sensory consequences of self-movement. CD signals provide the substrate for distinguishing self-caused from externally caused sensory change, with characteristic latencies and circuit motifs documented across modalities.
mapping.ree_translation: SD-003-prereq is the architectural-substrate claim that the REE forward-model comparator pipeline (E2_x for any reafferent stream z_x) requires an efference-copy distribution of a_actual to the forward model. Crapse and Sommer evidence that this architectural pattern is biologically real and species-general in primates: the brain implements exactly the substrate MECH-256 abstracts -- copies of action commands routed to predictive structures. The mapping is direct at the substrate level: a_actual in MECH-256 corresponds to the efference-copy / corollary discharge signal, and E2_x corresponds to the predictive structure receiving CD. SD-029's z_harm_s comparator is one specific instantiation across one specific stream; this paper evidences that the general pattern is biologically pervasive.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper supports the substrate (CD distribution + predictive structures) and the single-pass comparator output (predicted minus observed reafference). It does not directly evidence (a) the latent-level abstraction REE works with -- z_x rather than raw sensory afferents, (b) the two-pass counterfactual extension SD-003 originally proposed, or (c) the specific claim that a harm-stream (z_harm_s) has its own comparator. Transfer risk is moderate because primate motor CD circuits are well characterised, but the extension to interoceptive/harm streams is by analogy.
mapping.source_context: Review of primate (mostly macaque) single-unit, lesion, microstimulation, and tracing studies. Covers saccadic system (FEF, SC, MD thalamus), vocalisation (auditory cortex receiving laryngeal CD), reaching (parietal updating), and brief comments on rodent and bird homologues. Computational framing throughout.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality is high -- canonical review by the lab that did much of the saccadic CD work, peer-reviewed Elsevier neuroscience venue. Mapping fidelity is high at the substrate level (CD = efference copy = a_actual route) but moderates because the paper does not commit to a residual-comparator formalism and does not address interoceptive streams. Transfer risk is moderate -- primate motor CD generalises well across species, but the leap to a harm-stream comparator (SD-029) is an extension this paper does not directly endorse.
